critic_raw: The design uses class-weight balancing for both models to handle imbalance (0.1-0.3% fraud rate). But the primary metric is precision at fixed recall (0.80 recall). If class weights are tuned to optimize the primary metric on validation, and the primary metric is a specific operating point, different class weight schemes might achieve the same recall at different precisions. The design doesn't specify how class weights are tuned — is it grid search over class weight values on the validation set, or is it set to 1/fraud_rate?
defender_raw: Round 1: Class weights are 'applied to handle imbalance' — the exact weighting scheme (1/fraud_rate or tuned grid search) is an implementation detail that should be specified. For the sequence model, 'cross-entropy loss with class-weight balancing' suggests training-time balancing, while the primary metric evaluation is threshold-independent (the PR curve covers all thresholds). The recall threshold (0.80) is set post-hoc by reading the PR curve on validation. Class weight tuning is part of the hyperparameter search for both models. The design specifies equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort for both, so class weights are part of this search space for both models. This is an implementation note that should be documented but doesn't undermine the design.

rounds.critic_followup: If class weights are tuned via grid search for both models using the validation set, and the primary metric is precision at 0.80 recall on the validation set, then class weights are effectively tuned to maximize precision at recall=0.80. This could cause both models to overfit to this specific operating point on the validation distribution. The test set would then evaluate generalization to this specific calibration.
rounds.defender_followup: Class weights are a training-time parameter that affects the model's learned decision function. Tuning class weights to maximize validation precision at 0.80 recall is equivalent to optimizing for the desired operating point — this is appropriate behavior for a model being deployed at this operating point. If the optimal class weight generalizes from validation to test (i.e., the fraud prevalence is similar between validation and test windows), the test evaluation at 0.80 recall is valid. The design includes 'stratification by fraud prevalence across windows confirms similar fraud rates' — this check validates that the calibration generalizes.

rounds.critic_followup: The validation set is only 3 months (months 13-15). If fraud prevalence is 0.1-0.3%, then with typical card transaction volumes (millions per month), the validation set has thousands of fraud cases — sufficient for stable PR curve estimation. But for the 0.80 recall threshold itself: at 0.80 recall, the model may be detecting fraud events that are rare even in the validation set. Is 3 months of validation data sufficient for stable precision estimation at this operating point?
rounds.defender_followup: For a retail bank with millions of transactions per month at 0.1-0.3% fraud rate, 3 months of validation data contains 3M+ transactions and 3,000-9,000 fraud cases. The PR curve at 0.80 recall would have 2,400-7,200 true positive fraud cases, providing stable precision estimates. Bootstrap confidence intervals are included for uncertainty quantification. Three months of validation data is sufficient for stable operating point estimation at this scale. The design correctly includes bootstrap CIs to quantify this uncertainty.

adjudication_raw: Two rounds on class weight optimization and validation set sufficiency for PR curve estimation. Both resolved: class weight tuning to operating point is appropriate; 3 months of high-volume transaction data provides sufficient fraud cases for stable PR estimation with bootstrap CIs. Verdict: defense_wins.
